news_summary: Today's technology breakthroughs showcase advances in quantum science, with discoveries in high-dimensional quantum light, real-time light control chips, accessible DIY quantum computers, and pioneering antimatter delivery services.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées technologiques d'aujourd'hui mettent en avant les progrès de la science quantique, avec des découvertes dans le domaine de la lumière quantique à haute dimension, des puces permettant de contrôler la lumière en temps réel, des ordinateurs quantiques accessibles à monter soi-même et des services novateurs de livraison d'antimatière.
news_summary_es: Los avances tecnológicos actuales ponen de manifiesto los progresos en la ciencia cuántica, con descubrimientos en el ámbito de la luz cuántica de alta dimensión, chips de control de la luz en tiempo real, ordenadores cuánticos de montaje propio al alcance de todos y servicios pioneros de entrega de antimateria.

articles.title: Scientists just found a hidden 48-dimensional world in quantum light
articles.summary: A routine quantum optics technique just revealed an extraordinary secret: entangled light can carry incredibly complex topological structures. Researchers found these hidden patterns reach up to 48 dimensions, offering a vast new “alphabet” for encoding quantum information. Unlike previous assumptions, this topology can emerge from a single property of light—orbital angular momentum.
articles.content:
Scientists at the University of the Witwatersrand in South Africa, working with collaborators from Huzhou University, have uncovered a surprising feature in one of the most widely used tools in quantum optics. They found that standard methods for producing entangled photons can contain previously unseen topological structures. In their experiments, these structures reached a record 48 dimensions and included more than 17,000 distinct topological signatures, creating a vast new "alphabet" for encoding stable quantum information.

In most quantum optics labs, entangled photons are generated using a technique called spontaneous parametric downconversion (SPDC). This process naturally creates entanglement in the spatial properties of light. The researchers discovered that within this spatial structure lies a hidden realm of high-dimensional topologies. These complex patterns could provide new ways to store and protect information, potentially making quantum systems more resistant to noise.

The team demonstrated this effect using the orbital angular momentum (OAM) of light, which can span from simple two-dimensional cases to extremely high dimensions. This flexibility allows for much richer structures than previously recognized.

Topology Emerges From a Single Property

The findings, published in Nature Communications, show that measuring the OAM of two entangled photons reveals an intrinsic topology, a fundamental feature of the entanglement itself. Because OAM can take on an unlimited range of values, the associated topologies can also extend to very high dimensions.

"We report a major advance in this work: we only need one property of light (OAM) to make a topology, whereas previously it was assumed that at least two properties would be needed -- usually OAM and polarization," says Professor Andrew Forbes from the Wits School of Physics. "The consequence is that since OAM is high-dimensional, so too is the topology, and this let us report the highest topologies ever observed."

The researchers also found that once the topology goes beyond two dimensions, it can no longer be described by a single number. Instead, a range of topological values is required, reflecting a much richer and more complex structure than standard optical systems.

A Discovery Hiding in Plain Sight

One of the most notable aspects of this breakthrough is how accessible it is. The required resources are already present in most quantum optics laboratories, meaning no specialized equipment or "quantum engineer" is needed to take advantage of the effect.

Pedro Ornelas explains, "You get the topology for free, from the entanglement in space. It was always there, it just had to be found."

Guided by Theory, Confirmed by Experiment

According to lead author Prof. Robert de Mello Koch from Huzhou University, identifying these structures was not straightforward. "In high dimensions it is not so obvious where to look for the topology. We used abstract notions from quantum field theory to predict where to look and what to look for -- and found it in the experiment!"

Toward More Robust Quantum Technologies

Although orbital angular momentum entanglement has been widely explored, it has often been considered fragile. The researchers now suggest that viewing it through the lens of topology could change that perspective. By leveraging these newly discovered structures, scientists may be able to develop more reliable quantum systems, opening the door to practical, real-world applications.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in quantum optics, revealing a hidden 48-dimensional topological structure in entangled light. This discovery offers a new, vast 'alphabet' for encoding quantum information, which could lead to more robust and noise-resistant quantum technologies. The impact is broad and meaningful for the future of quantum computing and information science, with clear substance and detailed context provided.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover hidden 48-dimensional structures in quantum light
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study quantum light to understand how particles of light, called photons, can be linked or "entangled". This entanglement is important for future technologies like quantum computers and secure communication.

**What happened** – Researchers from the University of the Witwatersrand and Huzhou University found that entangled photons created by a common method have hidden complex patterns called topologies. These patterns exist in up to 48 dimensions and include over 17,000 unique shapes. They discovered this by looking at a property of light called orbital angular momentum (OAM), which describes how light twists and spins.

**Impact** – This is important because it shows a much richer way to store and protect quantum information than scientists thought before. Usually, quantum information can be fragile, but these complex topologies could make quantum systems stronger against errors and noise. Also, this discovery does not require new tools—many labs can explore it with equipment they already have.

**What's next step** – Scientists will work on using these high-dimensional topological structures to build better quantum technologies. This could help develop more stable quantum computers and communication systems that work well in real-life conditions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have uncovered intricate 48-dimensional patterns in entangled light that could lead to stronger and more reliable quantum technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques découvrent des structures cachées en 48 dimensions dans la lumière quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient la lumière quantique pour comprendre comment des particules de lumière, appelées photons, peuvent être liées ou "intriquées". Cette intrication est importante pour les technologies futures comme les ordinateurs quantiques et la communication sécurisée.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université de Witwatersrand et de l’Université de Huzhou ont découvert que les photons intriqués créés par une méthode courante cachent des motifs complexes appelés topologies. Ces motifs existent jusqu’à 48 dimensions et comprennent plus de 17 000 formes uniques. Ils ont trouvé cela en étudiant une propriété de la lumière appelée moment angulaire orbital (MAO), qui décrit la façon dont la lumière tourne.

**Impact** – Cela montre une manière beaucoup plus riche de stocker et protéger l’information quantique que ce que l’on pensait auparavant. Habituellement, l’information quantique est fragile, mais ces topologies complexes pourraient rendre les systèmes quantiques plus résistants aux erreurs et au bruit. De plus, cette découverte ne nécessite pas de nouveaux outils – beaucoup de laboratoires peuvent l’explorer avec leur équipement actuel.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont chercher à utiliser ces structures topologiques en haute dimension pour construire de meilleures technologies quantiques. Cela pourrait aider à développer des ordinateurs et des systèmes de communication quantiques plus stables, efficaces dans des conditions réelles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les chercheurs ont découvert des motifs complexes en 48 dimensions dans la lumière intriquée qui pourraient rendre les technologies quantiques plus fortes et plus fiables.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren estructuras ocultas en 48 dimensiones en la luz cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian la luz cuántica para entender cómo las partículas de luz, llamadas fotones, pueden estar "entrelazadas". Este entrelazamiento es importante para tecnologías futuras como computadoras cuánticas y comunicaciones seguras.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Universidad de Witwatersrand y la Universidad de Huzhou encontraron que los fotones entrelazados creados por un método común tienen patrones complejos ocultos llamados topologías. Estos patrones alcanzan hasta 48 dimensiones e incluyen más de 17,000 formas únicas. Lo descubrieron al estudiar una propiedad de la luz llamada momento angular orbital (OAM), que describe cómo la luz gira.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque muestra una forma mucho más rica de almacenar y proteger la información cuántica que se pensaba antes. Normalmente, la información cuántica es frágil, pero estas topologías complejas podrían hacer que los sistemas cuánticos sean más fuertes contra errores y ruido. Además, este descubrimiento no necesita herramientas nuevas: muchos laboratorios pueden estudiarlo con el equipo que ya tienen.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos trabajarán en usar estas estructuras topológicas de alta dimensión para crear mejores tecnologías cuánticas. Esto podría ayudar a desarrollar computadoras y sistemas de comunicación cuánticos más estables que funcionen bien en condiciones reales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los investigadores encontraron patrones complejos en 48 dimensiones en la luz entrelazada que podrían hacer las tecnologías cuánticas más fuertes y confiables.
articles.image_prompt: An intricate, warm-toned abstract painting of two entwined luminous spirals representing entangled photons, each spiral composed of countless delicate loops and twists symbolizing 48-dimensional topological structures and 17,000 unique patterns, set against a soft, natural gradient background that evokes the subtle spatial properties of quantum light.

articles.title: Harvard engineers build chip that can twist and control light in real time
articles.summary: Scientists at Harvard have built a miniature device that can twist and tune light in real time. By rotating two stacked photonic crystals and adjusting their spacing with a tiny mechanical system, they can control how light’s “handedness” behaves. This allows the chip to distinguish between left- and right-circular polarized light with remarkable precision. The advance could lead to smarter sensors, faster communications, and new quantum technologies.
articles.content:
Researchers at the Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences (SEAS) have created a compact device that can actively control the "handedness" of light as it passes through it, also known as optical chirality. This is achieved by slightly rotating two specially engineered photonic crystal layers.

The project was led by graduate student Fan Du in the lab of Eric Mazur, the Balkanski Professor of Physics and Applied Physics. The team designed a reconfigurable twisted bilayer photonic crystal that can be adjusted in real time using an integrated micro-electromechanical system (MEMS). This advance could enable new capabilities in chiral sensing, optical communication, and quantum photonics.

"Chirality is very important in many fields of science -- from pharma to chemistry, biology, and of course, physics and photonics," Mazur said. "By integrating twisted photonic crystals with MEMS, we have a platform that is not only powerful from a physics standpoint but also compatible with the way modern photonics are manufactured."

Twisted Photonic Crystals and Light Manipulation

Photonic crystals are nanoscale materials designed to control how light behaves. These structures, small enough to fit on the tip of a pin, are already used in technologies for computing, sensing, and high-speed data transmission.

Mazur's group has expanded this field by applying ideas from twistronics, a concept that gained attention through research on twisted bilayer graphene. By stacking two patterned silicon nitride layers and rotating them relative to each other, the researchers can create new optical properties that do not exist in a single layer.

In their study published in Optica, the team demonstrates that this twisted bilayer structure naturally introduces asymmetry between left and right, making it highly effective for controlling light chirality. Chirality refers to objects that cannot be superimposed on their mirror images, like left and right hands. In optics, this concept applies both to materials and to light itself, which can travel in a helical pattern.

Light can rotate clockwise, known as right-circular polarization, or counter-clockwise, known as left-circular polarization. While these differences are subtle, they play a critical role in many scientific applications.

Why Chirality Matters in Science

Small differences in chirality can have major consequences. In chemistry and medicine, molecules that are mirror images of each other can behave very differently in the body. A well-known example is thalidomide, a drug from the 1950s. One version of the molecule helped treat morning sickness in pregnant women, while its mirror image caused serious birth defects.

Scientists often use chiral light to study such molecules. Traditional tools, including wave plates and linear polarizers, can detect polarization but are fixed in their capabilities and limited in range.

Tunable Photonic Device With MEMS Control

The new Harvard device overcomes these limitations by being fully tunable. Instead of relying on static components, its response to different types of chiral light can be adjusted continuously without replacing any parts.

This flexibility comes from its bilayer design. When the two photonic crystal layers are brought close together and rotated, the structure becomes geometrically chiral and capable of detecting the handedness of incoming light. Strong interactions between the layers lead to very different transmission behaviors for left- and right-circularly polarized light under "normal incidence," or polarized light that hits perpendicular to the surface.

By using the MEMS system to precisely control both the twist angle and the spacing between layers, the researchers demonstrated that the device can be tuned to near-perfect selectivity when distinguishing light's handedness.

Future Applications in Sensing and Communications

The study also outlines a broader design strategy for creating twisted bilayer photonic crystals with controllable optical chirality. Although the current device serves as a proof of concept, it points toward practical applications.

Future systems could be used in chiral sensing, where devices are tuned to detect specific molecules at different wavelengths. They could also function as dynamic light modulators in optical communication systems, allowing precise control of light directly on a chip.

The paper, "Dynamic Control of Intrinsic Optical Chirality via MEMS-Integrated Photonic Crystals," was co-authored by Haoning Tang, Yifan Liu, Mingjie Zhang, Beicheng Lou, Guangqi Gao, Xuyang Li, Alsyl Enriquez, and Shanhui Fan.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough by Harvard engineers who developed a tunable photonic chip capable of real-time control of light chirality. This innovation has broad implications for improving sensors, optical communications, and quantum technologies, offering meaningful benefits to science and society. The article provides detailed context on the device's mechanism, significance, and potential applications, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Harvard engineers create chip that can twist and control light instantly
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have been working to control light in new ways to improve technology like sensors and communication devices. One tricky part is controlling light’s “handedness,” which means whether light twists to the left or right as it moves.

**What happened** – Researchers at Harvard built a tiny chip made of two layers of special materials called photonic crystals. By rotating these layers and adjusting the space between them using a small mechanical system, they can change how the chip controls the direction light twists, in real time.

**Impact** – This chip can tell the difference between left- and right-twisting light very precisely. This is important because light’s twist affects many fields, from medicine to physics. For example, certain medicines work differently depending on the molecule’s shape, which is related to chirality, the same idea as light’s handedness. The chip’s ability to adjust instantly means it could lead to better sensors, faster internet connections, and new quantum devices.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to develop this technology further to create practical devices for detecting specific molecules and improving communication systems. This could mean smarter medical tests and quicker data transfer using light.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Harvard engineers have built a small, adjustable chip that controls the twisting direction of light, opening doors to advanced sensors and faster communication technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des ingénieurs de Harvard créent une puce capable de contrôler la lumière en temps réel
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent à mieux contrôler la lumière pour améliorer des technologies comme les capteurs et les communications. Un défi est de maîtriser la « main » de la lumière, c’est-à-dire si elle tourne vers la gauche ou la droite en se déplaçant.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Harvard ont fabriqué une petite puce composée de deux couches de cristaux photoniques spéciaux. En faisant tourner ces couches et en ajustant leur distance grâce à un système mécanique minuscule, ils peuvent modifier la façon dont la puce contrôle la direction de la torsion de la lumière, en temps réel.

**Impact** – Cette puce distingue très précisément la lumière qui tourne à gauche ou à droite. C’est important car la torsion de la lumière joue un rôle dans plusieurs domaines, de la médecine à la physique. Par exemple, certains médicaments agissent différemment selon la forme des molécules, liée à la chiralité, qui est aussi la propriété de la lumière. La capacité d’ajustement instantané de la puce pourrait permettre de meilleurs capteurs, des communications plus rapides et de nouveaux appareils quantiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs veulent développer cette technologie pour fabriquer des dispositifs capables de détecter des molécules spécifiques et d’améliorer les systèmes de communication. Cela pourrait conduire à des tests médicaux plus efficaces et à un transfert de données plus rapide grâce à la lumière.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des ingénieurs de Harvard ont créé une petite puce réglable qui contrôle la direction de la torsion de la lumière, ouvrant la voie à des capteurs avancés et à des technologies de communication plus rapides.
articles.personality_title_es: Ingenieros de Harvard crean chip que controla la luz en tiempo real
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan nuevas formas de controlar la luz para mejorar tecnologías como sensores y comunicaciones. Un reto es controlar la “mano” de la luz, es decir, si gira hacia la izquierda o hacia la derecha mientras se mueve.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Harvard construyeron un chip pequeño hecho de dos capas de cristales fotónicos especiales. Al girar estas capas y ajustar el espacio entre ellas con un sistema mecánico pequeño, pueden cambiar cómo el chip controla la dirección del giro de la luz, en tiempo real.

**Impacto** – Este chip puede distinguir con mucha precisión la luz que gira a la izquierda o a la derecha. Esto es importante porque el giro de la luz afecta muchos campos, desde la medicina hasta la física. Por ejemplo, algunos medicamentos funcionan diferente según la forma de las moléculas, que está relacionada con la quiralidad, la misma idea que la mano de la luz. La capacidad de ajustar el chip al instante podría llevar a mejores sensores, comunicaciones más rápidas y nuevos dispositivos cuánticos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean mejorar esta tecnología para crear dispositivos prácticos que detecten moléculas específicas y mejoren los sistemas de comunicación. Esto podría significar pruebas médicas más inteligentes y una transferencia de datos más rápida usando luz.

**Resumen en una frase** – Ingenieros de Harvard han creado un chip pequeño y ajustable que controla la dirección del giro de la luz, abriendo paso a sensores avanzados y tecnologías de comunicación más rápidas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of two intricately patterned, semi-transparent crystal layers gently twisting over each other like interlocking spirals, with soft beams of light weaving through them in clockwise and counterclockwise helical paths, symbolizing controlled optical chirality and dynamic light manipulation.

articles.title: You can now buy a DIY quantum computer
articles.summary: Qilimanjaro is selling a relatively cheap kit with everything you need for a quantum computer – you just need to be able to put it together
articles.content:
Qilimanjaro is selling a relatively cheap kit with everything you need for a quantum computer – you just need to be able to put it together

Two engineers work on one of Qilimanjaro’s quantum computers Qilimanjaro

Quantum computers once seemed like fanciful machines of the future. Now, a DIY kit means that anyone with enough money and engineering skills can have one of their own.

Barcelona-based quantum computing company Qilimanjaro created EduQit by taking a “flatpack furniture” approach – gathering all the parts and giving customers the job of putting them together.

EduQit includes a chip made from tiny superconducting circuits, which is the heart of the quantum computer. There is also a special refrigerator that the chip is installed and wired into, along with a set of electronic devices that use radio waves and microwaves for controlling the chip and reading the results of its computations. All of this is combined with a smattering of racks, power cables and other devices that help complete the quantum computer.

Putting it all together isn’t a trivial task, but EduQit does come with instructions. Marta Estarellas at Qilimanjaro says the team offers training from its researchers and support throughout the building process. The training would take up to three months, she says, with the whole system being ready to run after at least 10 months of work.

The EduQit quantum computer comes with five qubits, which makes it less than a tenth of the size of cutting-edge devices, but it also only costs around €1 million, making it much cheaper. Most quantum computers are currently built by either tech giants or particularly well-funded start-ups and research institutions. For comparison, Google has said that it aims to bring its component costs down by a factor of 10 to bring the price of a single machine below $1 billion.

A Qilimanjaro quantum chip Qilimanjaro

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

Smaller-scale commercial machines are already for sale, but don’t come as a complete kit. For example, California-based company Rigetti sells a small superconducting quantum computer for research and development starting at about $900,000 for just the main chip and a few small components, roughly analogous to buying only the motherboard of a classical computer but not the monitor or the keyboard. Any research team that acquires one would have to buy the rest of the components by themselves.

Qilimanjaro is aiming the kit at the many research institutions where a lack of resources puts quantum computing technology out of reach. The firm is particularly focused on how it could give the next generation of researchers direct experience of building and running it.

Students can currently access quantum computers through the cloud or by working with computer simulations of quantum systems, but EduQit will allow them to develop more hands-on skills, says Estrellas. In this way, EduQit could become a quantum equivalent of a Raspberry Pi, a small and easy-to-modify computer that started as an education tool, but became broadly used among tinkerers and scientists.

Quantum computers promise to tackle calculations that are intractable even for the world’s best supercomputers, ranging from breaking the encryption that secures the internet to simulating the behaviour of molecules to discover new drugs. However, the chips are fragile and prone to errors. Building quantum computers that can realise this potential hinges on finding better ways to protect and control them.

A quantum computer comparable to EduQit would have been competitive with the most advanced devices available in some of the most sophisticated labs around a decade ago. The fact that it can be sold as a DIY kit shows how much quantum computing has advanced in the intervening years.

Katia Moskovitch at the firm Quantum Machines says there are many open questions for the future of quantum computing and the more people get a chance to play with them, the more likely it is that answers will be found.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant technological advancement making quantum computing more accessible through an affordable DIY kit. This democratizes access to cutting-edge technology, enabling broader research and education opportunities, which can accelerate innovation and development in the field with broad societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: You can now build your own quantum computer with an affordable kit
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Quantum computers are special machines that can solve problems much faster than regular computers. Until now, they were very expensive and hard to get, mostly only available to big companies or well-funded labs.

**What happened** – A company in Barcelona called Qilimanjaro has created EduQit, a do-it-yourself kit that lets people build their own quantum computer. The kit includes all the parts needed, like a special chip, a tiny refrigerator, and electronic controls. Buyers just need to assemble it following instructions and get training from Qilimanjaro’s experts.

**Impact** – EduQit costs about €1 million, which is much cheaper than other quantum computers that can cost hundreds of millions or even billions of dollars. Although it has fewer parts (five qubits), it can help many universities and researchers who couldn’t afford quantum computers before. It also gives students hands-on experience, which is important for learning and making new discoveries.

**What's next step** – After building the kit, users will spend months learning how to run and control the quantum computer. Qilimanjaro hopes this kit will become popular in education and research, helping more people experiment with quantum technology and find new solutions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Qilimanjaro’s EduQit kit makes quantum computing more affordable and accessible by letting people build and use their own machines.
articles.personality_title_fr: Vous pouvez maintenant construire votre propre ordinateur quantique avec un kit abordable
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ordinateurs quantiques sont des machines spéciales capables de résoudre des problèmes beaucoup plus rapidement que les ordinateurs classiques. Jusqu’à présent, ils étaient très coûteux et accessibles surtout aux grandes entreprises ou laboratoires bien financés.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une entreprise basée à Barcelone, Qilimanjaro, a créé EduQit, un kit à assembler soi-même pour construire son propre ordinateur quantique. Le kit contient toutes les pièces nécessaires, comme une puce spéciale, un petit réfrigérateur et des contrôles électroniques. Les acheteurs doivent simplement monter le kit en suivant les instructions et recevoir une formation par les experts de Qilimanjaro.

**Impact** – EduQit coûte environ 1 million d’euros, bien moins cher que les autres ordinateurs quantiques qui peuvent coûter des centaines de millions ou même des milliards de dollars. Bien qu’il ait moins de composants (cinq qubits), il permet à de nombreuses universités et chercheurs qui ne pouvaient pas se permettre ces machines d’y accéder. Il offre aussi aux étudiants une expérience pratique essentielle pour apprendre et faire de nouvelles découvertes.

**Prochaine étape** – Après avoir monté le kit, les utilisateurs passeront plusieurs mois à apprendre à faire fonctionner et contrôler l’ordinateur quantique. Qilimanjaro espère que ce kit deviendra populaire dans l’éducation et la recherche, aidant plus de personnes à expérimenter la technologie quantique et à trouver de nouvelles solutions.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le kit EduQit de Qilimanjaro rend l’informatique quantique plus abordable et accessible en permettant aux gens de construire et d’utiliser leurs propres machines.
articles.personality_title_es: Ahora puedes construir tu propia computadora cuántica con un kit asequible
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las computadoras cuánticas son máquinas especiales que pueden resolver problemas mucho más rápido que las computadoras normales. Hasta ahora, eran muy caras y estaban disponibles principalmente para grandes empresas o laboratorios con mucho dinero.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa en Barcelona llamada Qilimanjaro creó EduQit, un kit para armar tu propia computadora cuántica. El kit incluye todas las piezas necesarias, como un chip especial, un pequeño refrigerador y controles electrónicos. Los compradores solo deben ensamblarlo siguiendo las instrucciones y recibir capacitación de los expertos de Qilimanjaro.

**Impacto** – EduQit cuesta alrededor de 1 millón de euros, mucho más barato que otras computadoras cuánticas que pueden costar cientos de millones o incluso miles de millones de dólares. Aunque tiene menos partes (cinco qubits), puede ayudar a muchas universidades e investigadores que antes no podían permitirse estas máquinas. También ofrece a los estudiantes experiencia práctica, importante para aprender y hacer nuevos descubrimientos.

**Próximo paso** – Después de armar el kit, los usuarios pasarán varios meses aprendiendo a usar y controlar la computadora cuántica. Qilimanjaro espera que este kit se vuelva popular en la educación y la investigación, ayudando a más personas a experimentar con la tecnología cuántica y encontrar nuevas soluciones.

**Resumen en una frase** – El kit EduQit de Qilimanjaro hace que la computación cuántica sea más asequible y accesible al permitir que las personas construyan y usen sus propias máquinas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two stylized robotic hands carefully assembling a glowing, intricate quantum chip inside a transparent, softly illuminated miniature refrigerator, surrounded by delicate cables and circuit elements arranged like a flatpack kit on a wooden workbench bathed in gentle natural light.

articles.title: Inside the world’s first antimatter delivery service
articles.summary: On Tuesday, CERN will transport antiprotons on a truck for the first time, testing the plan to deliver antimatter by road to research labs across Europe
articles.content:
On Tuesday, CERN will transport antiprotons on a truck for the first time, testing the plan to deliver antimatter by road to research labs across Europe

The BASE-STEP transportable trap system Marina Cavazza, Chetna Krishna/CERN

Nestled in the heart of CERN’s antimatter factory, surrounded by intensely powerful magnetic fields and within a vacuum sparser than interstellar space, is some of the most sensitive material on Earth. Inside a filing-cabinet-sized box, which weighs a few hundred kilograms less than a Ford Focus, are a handful of antiprotons that have sat for weeks in extraordinary stillness. Most other particles produced in this building might expect to be probed and prodded, but these antiprotons have just one job: to sit tight and wait for their ride.

These hundred or so antimatter particles will soon be transported on the back of a truck around a 4-kilometre loop of road around the CERN campus, which will be the first demonstration of a future antimatter delivery service that will one day see antimatter transported to laboratories around Europe.

I have come to CERN’s campus, near Geneva, Switzerland, to see the experiment, called the Symmetry Tests in Experiments with Portable antiprotons (STEP), in its final preparations before the big day, as project leader Christian Smorra shows me around the facility.“It’s groundbreaking for antimatter science,” he says. “The idea of transporting antiprotons existed, in principle, since the time when this facility started, and now it’s the first time that it has become possible to actually do that.”

We have known since the 1920s that many particles have a near-identical counterpart, save for an opposite charge, called antimatter. But it took nearly a half-century for scientists to be able to produce and store the simplest antimatter – an antiproton – in significant quantities due to its propensity to annihilate and vanish when encountering its matter counterpart, the abundant proton.

The first experiments to confine antiprotons were carried out at CERN in the 1980s, where they were produced by smashing together protons into metal targets. Today, CERN’s Antimatter Decelerator hall, known as the antimatter factory, is the only place in the world that can produce millions of antiprotons on demand and store them for further study. It is home to seven different antimatter experiments, including the Baryon Antibaryon Symmetry Experiment (BASE), of which STEP is a part of.

Christian Smorra making the final touches David Stock

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

All these experiments are testing antimatter’s fundamental properties to extreme precision to see how it might deviate from regular matter. Any differences could shed light on why we appear to live in a matter-dominated universe, with a near-total absence of antimatter.

But to really probe down to the extraordinary precision required, it is necessary to filter out noisy radiation that might interfere with measurements, which is a problem for the antimatter factory. When antiprotons enter the hall, they are travelling at nearly the speed of light and must be slowed using powerful magnetic fields, which are impossible to fully block out.

In 2018, Smorra and his team realised that they would need to move antimatter away from the factory to somewhere quieter – and hatched an escape plan. “We had seen the impact of the magnetic field fluctuations, so it was clear that we would eventually need to continue our precision measurements [elsewhere],” says Smorra.

This wasn’t an easy task. Containing antimatter typically requires powerful magnetic fields produced by superconducting magnets, which need to be kept at near-absolute zero, requiring huge amounts of power. Smorra and his team designed STEP to use just a 30-litre tank of liquid helium to keep the magnets cool, so the electronics can instead run on a simple diesel generator. For the upcoming test run, though, it will use only battery power.

The magnet also has to be engineered to cope with the stop-start accelerations that occur while driving, as well as a bespoke vacuum system to ensure that the absence of problematic regular matter can be maintained while the antiprotons are loaded into and unloaded from the trap.

In 2024, Smorra and his team demonstrated that STEP works for regular protons by driving their contraption around the CERN campus on a truck. Now, Smorra and his team are about to try the real thing.

The preparations so far have been relatively straightforward. About a week before I arrived, around 100 antiprotons were slowed down and entered into the complex system of vacuums and electromagnetic fields that will hold them.

Since then, they have been sitting there idly at the centre of a tangle of wires and liquid helium pipes. Smorra and his team can check on their antimatter’s vital signs using a small oscilloscope screen affixed to the machine, where the characteristic frequency that antiprotons vibrate at takes the form of a two humps. They have affectionately pinned two googly eyes above each peak.

Signals showing the antiprotons are there David Stock

In the early hours of Tuesday morning, a crane will lift the entire 850-kilogram trap onto the back of a truck, driven by someone who will have had specialist training to drive CERN’s sensitive equipment around, making sure they don’t accelerate or stop too suddenly.

The truck will then take a 4-kilometre loop around the CERN campus, arriving back at the antimatter factory where it started.

If their test is successful, the eventual goal for Smorra and his team will be to drive their antimatter capsule on roads beyond CERN, delivering it to laboratories across Europe. One such facility is currently under construction at Heinrich Heine University Düsseldorf in Germany, where the antimatter will be studied in the absence of almost any external magnetic fields. However, this goal could take several years, as CERN will largely shut down in July to upgrade the Large Hadron Collider to operate at higher powers. That upgrade won’t finish until late 2028.

But once the antimatter delivery service is up and running, you could be driving down a Swiss or German motorway and find yourself next to a truck full of antimatter. It will look just like a normal truck, but its contents will be anything but normal. This might sound like a concerning proposition, given antimatter’s tendency to annihilate when it meets regular matter, but people shouldn’t be fearful, says Smorra.

“There’s nothing dangerous about the transport of antimatter, because the amount that we are transporting is so small,” says Smorra. “If you transport 1000 antiprotons and it gets lost, you won’t even notice it.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports a groundbreaking scientific and technological achievement: the first-ever road transport of antimatter, which enables precision experiments away from noisy environments and paves the way for broader antimatter research across Europe. This development has significant implications for fundamental physics and could lead to major scientific breakthroughs, benefiting the global scientific community and advancing knowledge about the universe. The story is focused, detailed, and describes a tangible, impactful event with broad scientific importance.
articles.personality_title: CERN tests the world’s first antimatter delivery by truck
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Antimatter is a rare type of particle that is the opposite of normal matter. Scientists study antimatter to understand why the universe is mostly made of matter. CERN, a famous research center near Geneva, Switzerland, produces and stores antimatter, but it has been hard to move it safely.

**What happened** – On Tuesday, CERN conducted the first-ever road transport of antimatter by loading about 100 antiprotons into a special container on a truck. The truck drove a 4-kilometer loop around the CERN campus. This experiment tested a new system called STEP, designed to keep antimatter stable during transport using powerful magnets cooled by liquid helium.

**Impact** – This test is important because it shows that antimatter can be moved safely outside the lab. Moving antimatter away from the noisy magnetic fields at CERN allows scientists to perform more precise experiments in quieter locations. This could help answer big questions about the universe and why antimatter is so rare.

**What's next step** – If the test is successful, CERN plans to deliver antimatter to other research labs across Europe, like one being built in Germany. This will take several years, partly because CERN will shut down for upgrades until 2028. Eventually, trucks carrying tiny amounts of antimatter might travel on public roads, but the amounts are so small they pose no danger.

**One-sentence takeaway** – CERN’s first road transport of antimatter proves it can be safely moved to new labs, opening the door for more detailed studies of this mysterious substance.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le CERN teste la première livraison d’antimatière par camion
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’antimatière est un type rare de particule, opposée à la matière normale. Les scientifiques l’étudient pour comprendre pourquoi l’univers est principalement composé de matière. Le CERN, un centre de recherche près de Genève, produit et stocke de l’antimatière, mais il est difficile de la déplacer en toute sécurité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Mardi, le CERN a réalisé le premier transport routier d’antimatière en chargeant environ 100 antiprotons dans un conteneur spécial sur un camion. Le camion a parcouru une boucle de 4 kilomètres autour du campus du CERN. Cette expérience a testé un nouveau système appelé STEP, conçu pour maintenir l’antimatière stable pendant le transport grâce à de puissants aimants refroidis à l’hélium liquide.

**Impact** – Ce test est important car il montre que l’antimatière peut être déplacée en toute sécurité hors du laboratoire. Éloigner l’antimatière des champs magnétiques perturbateurs du CERN permet aux scientifiques de faire des expériences plus précises dans des lieux plus calmes. Cela pourrait aider à répondre à de grandes questions sur l’univers et la rareté de l’antimatière.

**Prochaine étape** – Si le test réussit, le CERN prévoit de livrer de l’antimatière à d’autres laboratoires de recherche en Europe, comme un site en construction en Allemagne. Cela prendra plusieurs années, en partie parce que le CERN sera fermé pour des améliorations jusqu’en 2028. Un jour, des camions transportant de très petites quantités d’antimatière pourraient circuler sur les routes publiques, mais ces quantités sont trop faibles pour représenter un danger.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le premier transport routier d’antimatière par le CERN prouve qu’elle peut être déplacée en toute sécurité vers de nouveaux laboratoires, ouvrant la voie à des études plus approfondies de cette substance mystérieuse.
articles.personality_title_es: CERN prueba el primer transporte de antimateria por carretera
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La antimateria es un tipo raro de partícula que es opuesta a la materia normal. Los científicos la estudian para entender por qué el universo está formado principalmente por materia. CERN, un centro de investigación cerca de Ginebra, Suiza, produce y almacena antimateria, pero ha sido difícil moverla de forma segura.

**Qué pasó** – El martes, CERN realizó el primer transporte por carretera de antimateria al cargar cerca de 100 antiprotones en un contenedor especial sobre un camión. El camión recorrió un circuito de 4 kilómetros alrededor del campus de CERN. Esta prueba usó un sistema llamado STEP, diseñado para mantener la antimateria estable durante el transporte con potentes imanes enfriados con helio líquido.

**Impacto** – Esta prueba es importante porque muestra que la antimateria puede moverse fuera del laboratorio de manera segura. Alejar la antimateria de los campos magnéticos ruidosos en CERN permite a los científicos hacer experimentos más precisos en lugares más tranquilos. Esto podría ayudar a responder grandes preguntas sobre el universo y por qué la antimateria es tan rara.

**Próximo paso** – Si la prueba tiene éxito, CERN planea entregar antimateria a otros laboratorios en Europa, como uno que se está construyendo en Alemania. Esto tomará varios años, en parte porque CERN cerrará para mejoras hasta 2028. Algún día, camiones con pequeñas cantidades de antimateria podrían viajar por carreteras públicas, pero las cantidades son tan pequeñas que no representan peligro.

**Resumen en una frase** – El primer transporte por carretera de antimateria de CERN demuestra que puede moverse de forma segura a nuevos laboratorios, abriendo la puerta a estudios más detallados de esta sustancia misteriosa.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a sturdy scientific transport truck gently driving along a quiet, winding road encircling a futuristic research campus, carrying a compact, glowing magnetic trap softly illuminated from within to symbolize delicate antimatter safely contained, with subtle swirling lines representing invisible magnetic fields and a faint vacuum aura enveloping the device.
